My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
# Access the stored variable
business_data = var_functions.query_db_8

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for business in business_data:
    if isinstance(business.get('attributes'), dict):
        if business['attributes'].get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
            credit_card_businesses.append(business)

# Extract categories from description
categories = []
for business in credit_card_businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    # Find the part after "services in" or similar
    if 'services in' in desc:
        parts = desc.split('services in')
        if len(parts) > 1:
            category_text = parts[1]
            # Clean up the text
            category_text = category_text.replace('.', '').strip()
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            for sep in [',', ' and ']:
                category_text = category_text.replace(sep, ',')
            cats = [c.strip() for c in category_text.split(',') if c.strip()]
            categories.extend(cats)
    elif 'including' in desc:
        parts = desc.split('including')
        if len(parts) > 1:
            category_text = parts[1]
            category_text = category_text.replace('.', '').strip()
            for sep in [',', ' and ']:
                category_text = category_text.replace(sep, ',')
            cats = [c.strip() for c in category_text.split(',') if c.strip()]
            categories.extend(cats)

# Count unique categories
from collections import Counter
category_counts = Counter(categories)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses),
    "category_counts": dict(category_counts.most_common(10))
}))

import json
# Access the stored variable
business_data = var_functions.query_db_8

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for business in business_data:
    if isinstance(business.get('attributes'), dict):
        if business['attributes'].get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
            credit_card_businesses.append(business)

# Extract categories from description
categories = []
for business in credit_card_businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    # Find the part after "services in" or similar
    if 'services in' in desc:
        parts = desc.split('services in')
        if len(parts) > 1:
            category_text = parts[1]
            # Clean up the text
            category_text = category_text.replace('.', '').strip()
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            for sep in [',', ' and ']:
                category_text = category_text.replace(sep, ',')
            cats = [c.strip() for c in category_text.split(',') if c.strip()]
            categories.extend(cats)
    elif 'including' in desc:
        parts = desc.split('including')
        if len(parts) > 1:
            category_text = parts[1]
            category_text = category_text.replace('.', '').strip()
            for sep in [',', ' and ']:
                category_text = category_text.replace(sep, ',')
            cats = [c.strip() for c in category_text.split(',') if c.strip()]
            categories.extend(cats)

# Count unique categories
from collections import Counter
category_counts = Counter(categories)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses),
    "category_counts": dict(category_counts.most_common(10))
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load business and review data
business_data = var_functions.query_db_32  # All business data
review_file = var_functions.query_db_26    # Review data file path

# Parse review data
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    review_data = json.load(f)

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for business in business_data:
    if isinstance(business.get('attributes'), dict) and business['attributes'].get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        credit_card_businesses.append(business)

# Extract categories and count
category_counts = Counter()
business_categories = {}  # Map business_id to categories

for business in credit_card_businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., "businessid_49"
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract categories from description
    categories = []
    
    # Look for "services in" pattern
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = description.split('services in')[1]
        # Remove location info and common prefixes
        match = match.split('.')[0] if '.' in match else match
        match = match.split(' offering ')[0] if ' offering ' in match else match
        
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part and not part.startswith('including'):
                # Clean up the category
                part = re.sub(r'^in\s+', '', part)
                part = re.sub(r'^this\s+\w+\s+offers?\s+', '', part)
                if part:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Also look for "including" pattern
    elif 'including' in description:
        match = description.split('including')[1]
        match = match.split('.')[0] if '.' in match else match
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Also extract direct category lists at end of description
    # Pattern like: "fields of Cosmetics & Beauty Supply, Cosmetic Dentists, ..."
    if 'fields of' in description:
        match = description.split('fields of')[1]
        match = match.split('.')[0] if '.' in match else match
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Store categories for this business
    business_categories[business_id] = categories
    
    # Update counts
    for cat in categories:
        category_counts[cat] += 1

# Find the category with most businesses
if category_counts:
    top_category, top_count = category_counts.most_common(1)[0]
else:
    top_category, top_count = None, 0

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "top_category": top_category,
    "business_count_in_category": top_count,
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses)
}))

import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Load business data
business_data = var_functions.query_db_38

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for business in business_data:
    if isinstance(business.get('attributes'), dict) and business['attributes'].get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        credit_card_businesses.append(business)

# Extract categories from business descriptions
category_counts = Counter()
business_to_categories = {}  # Map business_id to list of categories
business_ids_in_category = defaultdict(list)  # Map category to list of business_ids

for business in credit_card_businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']  # e.g., "businessid_49"
    description = business.get('description', '')
    categories = []
    
    # Method 1: Look for "services in" pattern
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = description.split('services in')[1]
        # Clean up
        match = match.split('.')[0] if '.' in match else match
        match = match.split(' offering ')[0] if ' offering ' in match else match
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part and not any(skip in part for skip in ['providing', 'this facility', 'this establishment']):
                # Clean common prefixes
                part = re.sub(r'^in\s+', '', part)
                part = re.sub(r'^(this\s+)?\w+\s+(offers?|provides?|offering)\s+', '', part)
                if part and len(part) > 2:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 2: Look for "including" pattern  
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        # Try to get the part after including but before a period or location reference
        match = description.split('including')[1]
        match = match.split('.')[0] if '.' in match else match
        match = match.split(' and ')[0] if ' and ' in match and len(match.split(' and ')) > 3 else match
        # Split by commas
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', match)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part and 'and' not in part.lower().split()[-3:]:  # Skip if ends with "and"
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 3: Look for "fields of" pattern
    if 'fields of' in description:
        match = description.split('fields of')[1]
        match = match.split('.')[0] if '.' in match else match
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Fallback: Look for simple comma-separated lists
    if not categories and ',' in description:
        # Find parts that look like categories (after "services", "including", etc.)
        sentences = description.split('.')
        for sentence in sentences:
            if any(trigger in sentence for trigger in ['services', 'including', 'offers', 'provides']):
                # Extract potential category list
                parts = re.split(r',\s*', sentence)
                for part in parts:
                    part = part.strip()
                    if part and not any(word in part.lower() for word in ['located', 'this', 'offers', 'services', 'provides', 'range']):
                        categories.append(part)
    
    # Clean categories
    cleaned_categories = []
    for cat in categories:
        cat = cat.strip()
        # Remove trailing "and"
        cat = re.sub(r'\sand$', '', cat).strip()
        # Remove "and" from beginning
        cat = re.sub(r'^and\s+', '', cat).strip()
        if cat and len(cat) > 2:
            cleaned_categories.append(cat)
    
    # Store categories for this business
    business_to_categories[business_id] = cleaned_categories
    
    # Update counts
    for cat in cleaned_categories:
        category_counts[cat] += 1
        business_ids_in_category[cat].append(business_id)

# Find the category with the most credit-card-accepting businesses
if category_counts:
    top_category, top_count = category_counts.most_common(1)[0]
else:
    top_category, top_count = None, 0

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "top_category": top_category,
    "business_count": top_count,
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses),
    "category_samples": dict(category_counts.most_common(10))
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Access business data
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:38']

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for business in business_data:
    if isinstance(business.get('attributes'), dict) and business['attributes'].get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        credit_card_businesses.append(business)

# Extract categories from business descriptions
category_counts = Counter()
business_to_categories = {}  # Map business_id to categories
business_ids_by_category = defaultdict(list)  # Map category to list of business_ids

for business in credit_card_businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    description = business.get('description', '')
    categories = []
    
    # Extract categories - look for patterns like "services in X, Y, and Z"
    # Pattern 1: "services in" followed by list
    if 'services in' in description:
        services_part = description.split('services in')[1]
        # Stop at period or next sentence
        services_part = services_part.split('.')[0]
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', services_part)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Clean up the part
            part = re.sub(r'^[^a-zA-Z]+', '', part)  # Remove non-letters at start
            part = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z]+$', '', part)  # Remove non-letters at end
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 2: "including" followed by list
    elif 'including' in description:
        include_part = description.split('including')[1]
        include_part = include_part.split('.')[0]
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', include_part)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Clean up
            part = re.sub(r'^[^a-zA-Z]+', '', part)
            part = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z]+$', '', part)
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 3: "fields of" followed by list  
    if 'fields of' in description:
        fields_part = description.split('fields of')[1]
        fields_part = fields_part.split('.')[0]
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', fields_part)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Clean up
            part = re.sub(r'^[^a-zA-Z]+', '', part)
            part = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z]+$', '', part)
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Store categories for this business
    business_to_categories[business_id] = categories
    
    # Update counts
    for cat in categories:
        category_counts[cat] += 1
        business_ids_by_category[cat].append(business_id)

# Find top category
if category_counts:
    top_category = category_counts.most_common(1)[0]
else:
    top_category = (None, 0)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "top_category": top_category[0] if top_category else None,
    "business_count": top_category[1] if top_category else 0,
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses),
    "category_samples": dict(category_counts.most_common(10)),
    "businesses_in_top": business_ids_by_category.get(top_category[0], []) if top_category[0] else []
}))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Access business data
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:38']

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for business in business_data:
    if isinstance(business.get('attributes'), dict) and business['attributes'].get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        credit_card_businesses.append(business)

# Extract categories from business descriptions
category_counts = Counter()
business_to_categories = {}  # Map business_id to categories
business_ids_by_category = defaultdict(list)  # Map category to list of business_ids

for business in credit_card_businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    description = business.get('description', '')
    categories = []
    
    # Extract categories - look for patterns like "services in X, Y, and Z"
    # Pattern 1: "services in" followed by list
    if 'services in' in description:
        services_part = description.split('services in')[1]
        # Stop at period or next sentence
        services_part = services_part.split('.')[0]
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', services_part)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Clean up the part
            part = re.sub(r'^[^a-zA-Z]+', '', part)  # Remove non-letters at start
            part = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z]+$', '', part)  # Remove non-letters at end
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 2: "including" followed by list
    elif 'including' in description:
        include_part = description.split('including')[1]
        include_part = include_part.split('.')[0]
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', include_part)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Clean up
            part = re.sub(r'^[^a-zA-Z]+', '', part)
            part = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z]+$', '', part)
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 3: "fields of" followed by list  
    if 'fields of' in description:
        fields_part = description.split('fields of')[1]
        fields_part = fields_part.split('.')[0]
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', fields_part)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Clean up
            part = re.sub(r'^[^a-zA-Z]+', '', part)
            part = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z]+$', '', part)
            if part and len(part) > 2:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Store categories for this business
    business_to_categories[business_id] = categories
    
    # Update counts
    for cat in categories:
        category_counts[cat] += 1
        business_ids_by_category[cat].append(business_id)

# Find top category
if category_counts:
    top_category = category_counts.most_common(1)[0]
else:
    top_category = (None, 0)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "top_category": top_category[0] if top_category else None,
    "business_count": top_category[1] if top_category else 0,
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses),
    "category_samples": dict(category_counts.most_common(10)),
    "businesses_in_top": business_ids_by_category.get(top_category[0], []) if top_category[0] else []
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
